content:
Polynomial curve fitting predates modern machine learning. **Legendre** and **Chebyshev** polynomials (late 18th–19th century) were developed partly to approximate functions while controlling the numerical instability of high powers on intervals.

Classical **least squares**, formalized by **Gauss** and **Legendre** for astronomy and geodesy, already fit polynomials to noisy observations—the same normal equations used today.

In the 20th century, statisticians framed polynomial terms as **linear models with derived features**, separating "linear in parameters" from "linear in inputs." Response-surface methodology (Box & Wilson, 1950s) popularized quadratic models in designed experiments.

Modern ML libraries (`PolynomialFeatures`, Vandermonde utilities) automate expansion, but the core idea is unchanged: encode curvature explicitly, then let linear regression estimate coefficients.

timeline.label: Classical least squares
timeline.description: Fitting low-degree polynomials to astronomical and survey data established the normal-equation approach still used under the hood.

timeline.label: Orthogonal polynomials
timeline.description: Legendre and Chebyshev bases reduce numerical issues when fitting high-degree curves on bounded intervals.

timeline.label: Linear-model framing
timeline.description: Polynomial terms become engineered features; ridge/lasso and cross-validation address overfitting in high-degree fits.
